title: How Fusion Energy Is Finally Becoming Real

slides.heading: Fusion Energy Is Finally Becoming Real
slides.body: After 70 years of research, the power of the stars is within our reach

slides.heading: What Is Fusion?
slides.body: Fusion occurs when two light atomic nuclei — typically hydrogen isotopes — merge to form a heavier nucleus, releasing enormous energy. It's the same reaction that powers the Sun, and one small cup of fuel could power a home for centuries.

slides.heading: The 70-Year Quest
slides.body: Scientists have chased fusion since the 1950s. The old joke was that fusion is always '30 years away.' But breakthroughs in plasma physics, superconducting magnets, and AI-driven control systems have finally changed that calculus.

slides.heading: NIF's Historic Ignition
slides.body: In December 2022, the National Ignition Facility achieved 'ignition' — the fusion reaction produced more energy than the laser energy delivered to the fuel. It was the first time in history this milestone was reached, proving the physics works.

slides.heading: The Tokamak Approach
slides.body: Most fusion reactors use a donut-shaped chamber called a tokamak, which holds 100-million-degree plasma in place using powerful magnetic fields. ITER, a 35-nation project in France, is the world's largest tokamak — currently under final assembly.

slides.heading: Private Companies Race Ahead
slides.body: Over $6 billion in private capital has poured into fusion startups. Commonwealth Fusion Systems aims to fire up its compact SPARC reactor using revolutionary high-temperature superconducting magnets. TAE Technologies and Helion Energy are also closing in on net energy gain.

slides.heading: Why Fusion Beats Fission
slides.body: Unlike nuclear fission, fusion produces no long-lived radioactive waste, cannot cause a runaway meltdown, and its fuel — deuterium from seawater — is virtually limitless. It's the safest and cleanest form of nuclear energy by a wide margin.

slides.heading: The Remaining Challenges
slides.body: Sustaining plasma long enough to generate continuous power, handling extreme neutron bombardment on reactor walls, and making tritium fuel on-site are the key remaining hurdles. Engineers are attacking each problem with new materials and smarter designs.

slides.heading: When Can We Expect Fusion Power?
slides.body: Multiple credible timelines now point to pilot fusion power plants in the 2030s. Commonwealth Fusion and Helion both target demonstration reactors this decade. Commercial grid-scale fusion electricity could follow in the 2040s — within many people's lifetimes.

slides.heading: A Star in a Bottle
slides.body: Fusion energy would be transformative — abundant, clean, and safe. The science is proven. The engineering is advancing fast. The era of fusion power is not a dream anymore; it's an engineering sprint.
